title: Westerpark
standfirst: A massive Victorian gasworks converted into a cultural park — where industrial brick architecture meets wildflower meadows, indie cinemas, and techno clubs.
intro_html:
Westerpark is a story of two halves. On one side is a traditional, 19th-century residential neighborhood—red-brick houses, quiet canal-side walks, and local cafes. On the other is the Westergasfabriek, a former city gasworks built in the 1880s that was once the energy heart of Amsterdam. When the factory closed in 1967, it left behind a toxic landscape of contaminated soil and empty coal sheds.

The city's response was visionary. Instead of demolishing the historic industrial buildings, they were meticulously restored and surrounded by a vast, wild-green park. The coal storage units became a techno club, the purification plant became a luxury cinema, and the gas-holding tanks became the Fabrique des Lumières—a massive digital art exhibition space.

Westerpark is where Amsterdammers come to breathe. It’s less polished than the Vondelpark, more industrial, and significantly more creative. It feels like a neighborhood that actually belongs to the people who live here—a place of morning coffee, weekend markets, and late-night dancing among the Victorian brickwork.

todos.title: Westergasfabriek
todos.description:
The Westergasfabriek is the heart of the neighborhood, a repurposed industrial complex where the buildings themselves are as much an attraction as the businesses inside. The architecture is a stunning example of Dutch Neo-Renaissance industrial design—heavy brickwork, massive arched windows, and oxidized copper details.

Now a cultural hub, the complex houses everything from specialty coffee roasters to high-end restaurants and art galleries. It's a place where the city's industrial past and creative future collide, providing a backdrop for some of Amsterdam's most significant festivals, including the Sunday Market and Amsterdam Fashion Week.

todos.title: Fabrique des Lumières
todos.description:
Located in a former gas purifying hall, Fabrique des Lumières is a digital art center that uses cutting-edge projection technology to bring classic and contemporary art to life. The 17-meter-high brick walls serve as a massive canvas for immersive exhibitions where visitors are surrounded by light, color, and sound.

Walking through the space is like walking through a living masterpiece. Whether it's the swirling patterns of Van Gogh or the surrealist landscapes of Dalí, the scale and intensity of the projections create a sensory experience that is completely unique in the Amsterdam museum landscape. It's a high-tech tribute to the beauty of art in an unforgettable industrial setting.

todos.title: Het Ketelhuis
todos.description:
Het Ketelhuis is a small, independent cinema housed in a former boiler house that specializes in Dutch and European art-house films. The interior is cozy and unpretentious, with many of the original industrial features left exposed, creating an atmosphere that feels more like a private clubhouse than a commercial theater.

It is a champion of local cinematography, often hosting film festivals and director Q&As. The café on the ground floor is a local favorite, serving excellent coffee and house-made cake. It’s the perfect place to warm up on a rainy afternoon while supporting the city's independent film community.

todos.title: Tony’s Chocolonely Super Store
todos.description:
Located in the heart of the Westerpark, the Tony’s Chocolonely Super Store is more than just a chocolate shop—it's a vibrant, colorful, and slightly chaotic tribute to ethical chocolate making. This is the flagship store for the Dutch brand that is 'crazy about chocolate, serious about people.'

Visitors can learn about the company’s mission to make chocolate 100% slave-free, create their own customized chocolate bars, and sample a huge range of their famous un-evenly divided bars. It is a high-energy, sensory-overloaded experience that is both fun and socially conscious, making it a must-stop for anyone with a sweet tooth.

foods.title: Restaurant Amsterdam
foods.description:
Restaurant Amsterdam is located in the magnificent former engine room of a 19th-century water pumping station. The space is vast and soaring, with much of the original pumping machinery still in place, illuminated by high-industrial windows.

The menu focuses on classic European and Dutch brasserie fare—fresh seafood, high-quality steaks, and seasonal vegetables. Despite the grand setting, the vibe is surprisingly unpretentious and lively, making it a favorite for broad, shared dinners and sophisticated weekend lunches.

foods.title: Espressofabriek
foods.description:
Espressofabriek was one of the first specialty coffee roasters in Amsterdam and remains one of the best. Located in a bright, airy industrial space in the Westerpark, they roast their own beans on-site, filling the air with the rich aroma of fresh coffee.

The focus is entirely on the quality of the bean and the extraction. There’s no fancy deco or complicated syrups—just world-class espresso, flat whites, and filter coffees served in a minimalist, sun-drenched setting that spill out onto a massive park-facing terrace in the summer.

foods.title: Pacific
foods.description:
Pacific is a chameleon of a venue located at the edge of the Westergasfabriek. During the day, it is a relaxed, colorful cafe with a massive sun-facing terrace that serves great burgers and artisanal sandwiches. By night, it transforms into a high-energy bar and dance venue.

It is one of the most inclusive and unpretentious spots in the park, where you’re just as likely to find families during the day as you are late-night revelers dancing to soul, funk, and techno. The vibe is permanently relaxed, welcoming, and slightly bohemian.

products.title: Fabrique des Lumières
products.description: Book your 1-hour immersive art experience. Limited slots for the current Van Gogh show.

products.title: Artisanal Food Tour
products.description: Cover the culinary highlights of Westerpark — from Tony's chocolate to local micro-breweries.

getting_there.text: Tram 5 to Van Hallstraat stop, or Bus 21 from Centraal Station.
10-minute cycle from the Anne Frank House.
